commands.review_summary:
The branch exports the shared state database best-effort chmod helper, uses it for agent database directory and sidecar hardening, and adds a chmod-failure regression test.

PR surface: Source +1, Tests +90. Total +91 across 3 files.

Reproducibility: yes. at source level: current main calls raw chmodSync on the per-agent DB open path, so an ENOTSUP/EPERM chmod-less filesystem error would propagate before the agent DB opens. I did not run a live chmod-less mount in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: - [P1] The remaining blocker is contributor real behavior proof or maintainer acceptance of mock-only proof, not a concrete code repair for ClawSweeper to queue.

target.title: fix(state): tolerate chmod-less volumes when opening the agent database
target.body:
## Summary

`ensureOpenClawAgentDatabasePermissions` in `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.ts` called raw `chmodSync` for the per-agent DB directory and sidecar files. Its sibling, the shared state DB in `src/state/openclaw-state-db.ts`, routes the identical hardening through `bestEffortChmodSync`, which was added in #91919 (commit 13dce48321) to keep the gateway alive on filesystems where `chmod` is unsupported (Azure Files, some Docker volume drivers, NFS without `no_root_squash`, certain k8s PVCs).

That fix only covered the shared state DB. The agent DB still crashed on the same volumes. For the default agent path `isDefaultAgentDatabase` is always true, so `chmodSync` runs on every open. The result on an affected volume: the gateway's shared state DB comes up fine, then the first agent operation (for example loading auth profiles) calls `openOpenClawAgentDatabase`, `chmodSync` throws `ENOTSUP`, and it is uncaught. The agent cannot load auth or run, while the gateway looks healthy, which makes the cause confusing. This is exactly the failure mode #91919 was fixed to support, one layer down.

## Fix

- Export `bestEffortChmodSync` from `openclaw-state-db.ts` (it already encapsulates the tolerate-or-throw policy).
- Use it for both the agent DB directory and the sidecar files in `openclaw-agent-db.ts`.

This preserves fail-closed behavior for ordinary `EPERM` ownership faults on POSIX filesystems: the helper only tolerates the unambiguous unsupported codes (`ENOTSUP`/`EOPNOTSUPP`/`EINVAL`) or a probe-confirmed `EPERM` where the filesystem itself rejects `chmod`. Unexpected codes such as `EACCES` still throw, keeping the private-mode hardening loud.

## Verification

Added colocated regression test `src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts`, mirroring the existing `openclaw-state-db.permissions.test.ts` from #91919. It mocks only `node:fs` `chmodSync` to simulate a chmod-rejecting volume and drives the real `openOpenClawAgentDatabase`.

Before/after on the same mocked filesystem:

```
# Before fix (raw chmodSync, source reverted, new test present):
 Test Files  1 failed (1)
      Tests  2 failed | 1 passed (3)
   x opens the agent database when chmodSync throws ENOTSUP
       Error: ENOTSUP: chmod failed
   x opens when the filesystem probe also rejects chmod with EPERM
       Error: EPERM: chmod failed
   ok rethrows unexpected chmod errors at open   (EACCES stays fatal on both)

# After fix:
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  3 passed (3)
```

Both permissions suites together stay green:

```
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/state/openclaw-state-db.permissions.test.ts src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts
 Test Files  2 passed (2)
      Tests  10 passed (10)
```

Checks run locally (clean source checkout):

- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p tsconfig.core.json` -> exit 0
- `node scripts/run-tsgo.mjs -p test/tsconfig/tsconfig.test.src.json` -> exit 0
- `node scripts/run-oxlint.mjs --tsconfig config/tsconfig/oxlint.core.json <changed files>` -> clean
- `oxfmt --check <changed files>` -> clean

### Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: Opening the per-agent SQLite database no longer crashes with ENOTSUP on filesystems that do not support chmod (Azure Files, some Docker volume drivers, NFS without no_root_squash, certain k8s PVCs); it tolerates the unsupported codes like the shared state DB does, while still failing closed on real permission faults.

Real environment tested: Linux (Node 22.19), OpenClaw repo checkout. The chmod-rejecting volume is simulated by mocking node:fs chmodSync to throw the volume's errno, the same technique the shipped #91919 test uses; the rest of the path (mkdir, sqlite open, schema, registration) runs for real against a temp state dir.

Exact steps or command run after this patch: node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/state/openclaw-agent-db.permissions.test.ts ; and the before/after comparison above by stashing only the two source files and re-running the same test.

Evidence after fix: Test Files 1 passed (1); Tests 3 passed (3) for the agent DB suite; 10 passed (10) for both permissions suites together. Before the fix, the two tolerance cases threw ENOTSUP and EPERM respectively.

Observed result after fix: openOpenClawAgentDatabase returns an open handle (db.isOpen === true) on the mocked chmod-rejecting volume, and still throws on EACCES.

What was not tested: A real Azure Files / NFS / Docker-volume mount end to end (simulated via the same fs mock as the shipped state-DB fix); full gateway boot on such a volume.

Refs: #91919 (the shared state DB fix this extends to the agent DB).
target.author: yetval
